planting.note: Plant container shrubs in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Anthony Waterer blooms on new wood — shear to 6–8 inches in late winter or very early spring to stimulate maximum new growth and flower production.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Anthony Waterer is the classic carmine-rose Japanese spiraea cultivar — one of the oldest and most widely grown selections of Spiraea japonica, introduced before 1884 and still among the most popular compact summer-blooming shrubs in American horticulture. The flat-topped, 3–4 inch flower clusters are a rich carmine-rose to deep rose-pink color from June through August (in zone 7–8), with peak bloom lasting 4–6 weeks followed by scattered repeat bloom if sheared lightly after the first flush. As with all Japanese spiraea cultivars, Anthony Waterer blooms on new wood — shear the entire plant hard to 6–8 inches in late winter or very early spring to stimulate vigorous new growth and produce the densest possible flower coverage. An established plant that is not sheared becomes leggy and produces fewer flowers; annual hard pruning is the most important maintenance practice for this species. Anthony Waterer grows to 3–4 feet tall and wide with a neat, rounded, mounding form that requires virtually no pruning to maintain shape beyond the annual renewal shearing. The new growth often emerges with distinctive cream and pink variegation, maturing to green. The plant is exceptionally adaptable — tolerating clay soil, heat, drought, and urban conditions throughout zone 9.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Vanilla Strawberry blooms on new wood — prune hard in late winter to stimulate vigorous new growth for maximum flower production.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Vanilla Strawberry is one of the most spectacular and distinctive panicle hydrangea cultivars — producing enormous, upright, conical flower panicles (up to 12–16 inches long) that undergo a dramatic color transformation through the growing season: opening pure creamy-white in early summer, then progressively blushing from the base of each panicle upward from pink to vivid strawberry-red as summer advances, creating a bicolor white-and-strawberry effect that is unlike any other hydrangea. At peak color in late summer and early fall, the large panicles display a gradient from strawberry-red at the base through pink to creamy-white at the tip — an extraordinary color combination that has made Vanilla Strawberry one of the fastest-selling new shrubs since its introduction. The shrub grows 6–8 feet tall and 4–6 feet wide with a strong, upright habit and sturdy stems that support the heavy flower panicles without flopping — an advantage over some other large-flowered H. paniculata types. Like all H. paniculata, it blooms on new wood and is pruned hard in late winter. In zone 9, morning sun with afternoon shade produces optimal results — the intense strawberry-red color develops best with some direct sun but the flowers hold color better if protected from the intense afternoon Gulf South sun.

common_name: Nuccio's Gems Camellia (Formal Double White Camellia)

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in fall or early spring in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Nuccio's Gems is a mid-season japonica blooming January through March in zone 9 — one of the most perfect formal double white camellias in cultivation.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade; Nuccio's Gems is one of the most celebrated and award-winning Camellia japonica cultivars ever introduced — developed by Nuccio's Nurseries in Altadena, California, and recognized by the American Camellia Society for its exceptionally formal, regular, perfectly arranged double flowers of pure white with a very faint pink blush at times in cool temperatures. The flowers are consistently formal double in form — every petal is precisely arranged in concentric rings with mathematical regularity, with no stamens visible and no disorder of petals regardless of weather conditions. This consistency of form across different temperatures and conditions is the defining quality of Nuccio's Gems and the characteristic most prized by camellia enthusiasts. The blooms are medium-large (3.5–4.5 inches), long-lasting, and borne prolifically over a mid-season period from January through March in zone 9. The upright-growing shrub develops excellent deep-green, lustrous foliage and a clean, compact form reaching 6–10 feet. Like all C. japonica cultivars in zone 9, plant in dappled shade or morning sun with afternoon shade — the flowers burn in full afternoon sun. Mulch heavily, maintain consistent moisture, and feed with acid-forming azalea fertilizer in March and June. The pure white, perfectly formal flowers of Nuccio's Gems are considered among the finest white camellias available to American gardeners.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–7.0) soil in partial shade when young, transitioning to full sun at maturity; Wells pairs well with Shenandoah or Susquehanna for cross-pollination — two cultivars are needed for reliable fruit production.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade when young, transitioning to full sun at fruiting age; Wells is a named pawpaw cultivar selected from a wild tree in Maryland — valued for its exceptionally large fruit size, excellent sweet flavor with classic banana-custard character, and good productivity. Wells produces some of the largest pawpaw fruits of any named cultivar, with individual fruits commonly weighing 6–12 ounces and occasionally larger — a significant attribute for a species where fruit size varies enormously between seedling selections. The large, heavy fruits have creamy-yellow flesh with minimal fiber, full banana-vanilla custard flavor, and a smooth texture that makes them outstanding for fresh eating and pawpaw-based recipes (ice cream, smoothies, bread, beer). Wells ripens mid-to-late season (late September to early October in zone 7–8, slightly earlier in zone 9) and requires cross-pollination from a different named cultivar such as Shenandoah or Susquehanna for maximum fruit set. The very short shelf life inherent to all pawpaw fruit (2–4 days at room temperature, up to 2 weeks refrigerated) limits commercial distribution but is not a problem for home gardeners harvesting on demand. Hand-pollination with a small brush during the brief flowering window significantly improves fruit set in home orchard settings where natural fly and beetle pollinators may be scarce.
sources: Kentucky State University Pawpaw Research Program

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Walker's Low is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established — it performs best in lean soils and declines rapidly in heavy, wet clay without amendment and drainage improvement.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Walker's Low is the Perennial Plant Association's 2007 Plant of the Year and one of the most reliable, versatile, and long-blooming perennials available for the full-sun border — producing a continuous display of small, tubular, lavender-blue flowers in dense whorls along arching stems from May through September (with shearing after the first main flush to stimulate repeat bloom). The aromatic gray-green foliage emits a pleasant minty-catmint fragrance when brushed or disturbed — similar to the scent of catnip, though Walker's Low is far less potent to cats than true catnip (Nepeta cataria). The mounding, gently arching form with a soft, hazy, lavender cloud effect is one of the most effective and widely used planting combinations in contemporary perennial garden design: Walker's Low paired with ornamental grasses, roses, and Russian sage creates a classic soft-textured, long-season color composition. Walker's Low is highly attractive to pollinators — bees of many species, hummingbirds, and butterflies visit constantly during the long bloom season. Shear to 6 inches after the main late-spring flush to stimulate dense new growth and a strong repeat bloom in late summer. In zone 9, plant in well-drained soil — Walker's Low will not tolerate standing water or consistently wet soil in the Gulf South summer heat.
